why_others_wrong.a: MCP tools are NOT always available. When an explicit tools allowlist is set, only listed tools are allowed. MCP tools would only be inherited if the tools field is omitted entirely.
why_others_wrong.c: The mcpServers field controls which MCP servers the subagent connects to, but the tools allowlist still governs which tools are available. Listing an MCP server doesn't override the tools restriction.
why_others_wrong.d: Subagents CAN access MCP tools — either by omitting the tools field (inheriting everything) or by explicitly listing MCP tools in the tools field.

doc_reference.quote: tools: Tools the subagent can use. Inherits ALL tools if omitted (including MCP). tools set → only those tools allowed.

question: Your organization sets CLAUDE_CODE_SUBAGENT_MODEL=claude-haiku-4-5 as an environment variable. A subagent definition specifies model: opus in its frontmatter. When the main conversation (running Sonnet) invokes this subagent, which model will it use?

options.text: Opus — the frontmatter model field takes highest priority
Sonnet — the main conversation's model is always inherited
Haiku 4.5 — the CLAUDE_CODE_SUBAGENT_MODEL environment variable takes highest priority
The request fails because of conflicting model specifications

explanation: Model resolution follows a strict priority order: (1) CLAUDE_CODE_SUBAGENT_MODEL environment variable (highest), (2) per-invocation model parameter, (3) subagent definition's model frontmatter, (4) main conversation's model. Since the environment variable is set to claude-haiku-4-5, it takes precedence over the frontmatter's 'opus' specification.

why_others_wrong.a: The frontmatter model field is priority 3, not highest. The CLAUDE_CODE_SUBAGENT_MODEL environment variable (priority 1) overrides it.
why_others_wrong.b: The main conversation's model is priority 4 (lowest). It would only be used if no other specification exists.
why_others_wrong.d: Conflicting model specifications do not cause errors. The resolution order is well-defined and the highest priority source wins.

doc_reference.quote: Model Resolution Order: 1. CLAUDE_CODE_SUBAGENT_MODEL environment variable (if set). 2. Per-invocation model parameter. 3. Subagent definition's model frontmatter. 4. Main conversation's model.

question: An organization deploys a 'security-scanner' subagent via managed settings. A developer also has a 'security-scanner' subagent in their project's .claude/agents/ directory. Which one takes effect and why?

options.text: The project subagent takes effect because project-level configurations override organizational settings
Both are loaded and Claude chooses which to use based on the task description
The managed settings subagent wins because it has highest priority (1), overriding the project subagent (priority 3)
The system reports a naming conflict error and neither subagent is loaded

explanation: When multiple subagents share the same name, the higher-priority location wins. The scope priority hierarchy is: Managed settings (1, highest) > --agents CLI flag (2) > .claude/agents/ (3) > ~/.claude/agents/ (4) > Plugin agents/ (5, lowest). The managed settings subagent at priority 1 overrides the project subagent at priority 3.

why_others_wrong.a: Project-level configurations do NOT override managed settings. Managed settings have the highest priority in the hierarchy, ensuring organizational policies take precedence.
why_others_wrong.b: When names conflict, only one subagent is loaded — the one from the highest-priority location. Claude does not choose between duplicates.
why_others_wrong.d: There is no naming conflict error. The system silently resolves conflicts using the priority hierarchy — the highest-priority definition wins.

doc_reference.quote: Scope priority: Managed settings (1, highest) > --agents CLI flag (2) > .claude/agents/ (3) > ~/.claude/agents/ (4) > Plugin agents/ (5, lowest). When multiple subagents share the same name, higher-priority location wins.

question: A parent conversation runs with permissionMode: 'acceptEdits'. A subagent's frontmatter sets permissionMode: 'default' to enforce stricter permission checks. During testing, the subagent auto-approves file edits without prompting. Why?

options.text: The subagent's frontmatter permissionMode was not saved correctly and defaulted to acceptEdits
If the parent uses acceptEdits or bypassPermissions, it takes precedence and cannot be overridden by the subagent's frontmatter
The default permissionMode inherits from the parent automatically, ignoring the frontmatter
File edits are always auto-approved in subagents regardless of permission mode

explanation: This is a critical permission inheritance rule: if the parent uses bypassPermissions or acceptEdits, it takes precedence and CANNOT be overridden by the subagent's frontmatter permissionMode. Similarly, if the parent uses auto mode, the subagent inherits it and the frontmatter permissionMode is ignored. This ensures that the parent's security posture is not weakened by subagent configurations.

why_others_wrong.a: The frontmatter was likely saved correctly, but it's being overridden by the parent's acceptEdits permission mode. This is by design, not a configuration error.
why_others_wrong.c: The default permissionMode does NOT always inherit from the parent. The inheritance only overrides in specific cases: when the parent uses bypassPermissions, acceptEdits, or auto. With other modes, the subagent's frontmatter takes effect.
why_others_wrong.d: File edits are NOT always auto-approved in subagents. They follow the effective permission mode, which in this case is inherited from the parent's acceptEdits.

doc_reference.quote: If parent uses bypassPermissions or acceptEdits: takes precedence, cannot be overridden. If parent uses auto mode: subagent inherits auto mode, permissionMode frontmatter ignored.

question: You install a Claude Code plugin that includes a subagent with hooks, mcpServers, and permissionMode configured in its frontmatter. After installation, the hooks and MCP servers don't work. What is the explanation?

options.text: Plugin subagents require a separate beta header to enable hooks and MCP server support
Plugin subagents silently ignore hooks, mcpServers, and permissionMode fields for security — copy the agent to .claude/agents/ to use these features
Plugin subagents only support hooks and MCP servers when running in bypassPermissions mode
The plugin's agents/ directory has lowest priority, so these features are overridden by default settings

explanation: Plugin subagents have security restrictions: they do NOT support hooks, mcpServers, or permissionMode frontmatter fields. These fields are silently ignored when the subagent comes from a plugin directory. To use these features, copy the agent file into .claude/agents/ (project) or ~/.claude/agents/ (user), where they are fully supported.

why_others_wrong.a: There is no beta header for plugin subagent features. The restriction is a deliberate security policy, not a feature gate.
why_others_wrong.c: Permission mode doesn't affect whether hooks and MCP servers work for plugin subagents. The restriction is absolute for all plugin-sourced subagents.
why_others_wrong.d: While plugins do have lowest priority (5), the issue is not about priority — it's about security restrictions that silently ignore these specific fields for plugin subagents.

doc_reference.quote: Plugin subagents do NOT support hooks, mcpServers, or permissionMode frontmatter fields (silently ignored). To use them, copy the agent file into .claude/agents/ or ~/.claude/agents/.

question: You configure a subagent with memory: 'project'. When the subagent starts, what happens with its memory?

options.text: The subagent loads the parent's MEMORY.md file from the project root
The system prompt includes the first 200 lines or 25KB of the subagent's own MEMORY.md from .claude/agent-memory/<name>/, and Read/Write/Edit tools are auto-enabled
The subagent creates a new empty memory file and waits for instructions to populate it
Memory is loaded from ~/.claude/agent-memory/<name>/ because project scope uses the user's home directory

explanation: When memory is enabled with scope 'project', the system prompt includes the first 200 lines or 25KB (whichever comes first) of the subagent's own MEMORY.md from .claude/agent-memory/<name>/. Read, Write, and Edit tools are automatically enabled for memory management. The memory is separate from the parent's memory — each subagent has its own memory space named after it.

why_others_wrong.a: Subagents do NOT load the parent's MEMORY.md. Each subagent has its own separate memory space at .claude/agent-memory/<name>/, isolated from the parent.
why_others_wrong.c: The subagent doesn't just create an empty file and wait. If MEMORY.md exists, it's loaded immediately. If it doesn't exist, the subagent can create and populate it during its work.
why_others_wrong.d: Project scope uses .claude/agent-memory/<name>/ (project directory), not the user's home directory. The 'user' scope uses ~/.claude/agent-memory/<name>/.

doc_reference.quote: project scope: .claude/agent-memory/<name>/. When enabled: includes first 200 lines or 25KB of MEMORY.md (whichever first); Read/Write/Edit tools auto-enabled.

question: You want a subagent to modify files without conflicting with the main conversation's work. Which frontmatter field creates an isolated copy of the repository for the subagent?

options.text: background: true — background subagents automatically get isolated file systems
isolation: worktree — creates a temporary git worktree for the subagent, auto-cleaned if no changes
permissionMode: plan — plan mode prevents file conflicts by making the subagent read-only
tools: Read, Grep — limiting tools to read-only prevents file modification conflicts

explanation: Setting isolation: worktree in the frontmatter gives the subagent a temporary git worktree — an isolated copy of the repository. This allows the subagent to make file changes without conflicting with other work. The worktree is automatically cleaned up if the subagent makes no changes, keeping the repository tidy.

why_others_wrong.a: Background subagents do NOT automatically get isolated file systems. They share the same working directory as the parent. isolation: worktree must be explicitly set.
why_others_wrong.c: Plan mode makes the subagent read-only, which prevents conflicts but also prevents the subagent from making any modifications at all. The requirement is to modify files without conflicts.
why_others_wrong.d: Limiting to read-only tools prevents all modifications, not just conflicting ones. The requirement is file modification in isolation, not prevention of all modifications.

doc_reference.quote: isolation: worktree for isolated git worktree copy. Gives the subagent an isolated copy of the repository, useful for parallel work without file conflicts. Auto-cleaned if no changes.

question: A subagent definition has both 'tools: Read, Write, Bash' and 'disallowedTools: Write' in its frontmatter. Which tools will the subagent actually have access to?

options.text: Read, Write, Bash — the tools allowlist takes precedence over disallowedTools
Read, Bash — disallowedTools is applied first, removing Write, then tools is resolved against the remaining set
This is an invalid configuration that produces an error on subagent creation
Only Write — disallowedTools inverts the tools list

explanation: When both tools and disallowedTools are set, disallowedTools is applied FIRST, removing the denied tools from the available set. Then tools is resolved against the remaining list. Since Write is in disallowedTools, it's removed first. Then the tools allowlist (Read, Write, Bash) is resolved, but Write has already been removed. The result is Read and Bash.

why_others_wrong.a: The tools allowlist does NOT take precedence over disallowedTools. The order of operations is: disallowedTools applied first, then tools resolved against the remaining set.
why_others_wrong.c: This is not an invalid configuration. Having both fields is supported — the documentation specifies the exact resolution order.
why_others_wrong.d: disallowedTools does not invert the tools list. It removes specific tools, and the remaining tools are available.

doc_reference.quote: If both set: disallowedTools applied first, then tools resolved against remaining. A tool in both is removed.

question: A colleague claims there are only 7 configurable fields for subagent frontmatter: name, description, tools, model, permissions, hooks, and memory. How many fields actually exist and what key fields are they missing?

options.text: There are 10 fields — they are missing disallowedTools, maxTurns, and skills
There are 16 fields — they are missing disallowedTools, permissionMode, maxTurns, skills, mcpServers, background, effort, isolation, color, and initialPrompt among others
There are 7 fields — their list is complete and correct
There are 20 fields — the additional fields were added in the latest release

explanation: There are exactly 16 supported frontmatter fields for subagent definitions. The complete list is: name, description, tools, disallowedTools, model, permissionMode, maxTurns, skills, mcpServers, hooks, memory, background, effort, isolation, color, and initialPrompt. The colleague's list of 7 is missing 9 important fields.

why_others_wrong.a: There are 16 fields, not 10. Many more fields beyond the three listed are missing from the original claim.
why_others_wrong.c: The list of 7 is incomplete. There are 16 total fields covering tool control, model selection, permissions, memory, background execution, effort levels, isolation, display, and initial prompting.
why_others_wrong.d: There are 16 fields, not 20. The documentation clearly lists exactly 16 supported frontmatter fields.

doc_reference.quote: All 16 Supported Frontmatter Fields: name, description, tools, disallowedTools, model, permissionMode, maxTurns, skills, mcpServers, hooks, memory, background, effort, isolation, color, initialPrompt.

question: You need to prevent Claude from ever executing 'rm -rf /' in your CI/CD pipeline, regardless of what the system prompt says or what prompt injection attacks might attempt. Which approach provides a 100% guarantee?

options.text: Add 'NEVER run rm -rf /' to the system prompt in bold
Use strict: true on the Bash tool definition with a restricted input schema
Implement a PreToolUse hook that checks for dangerous commands and returns exit code 2 to block them
Set tool_choice to 'none' for any conversation mentioning file deletion

explanation: Hooks provide 100% deterministic enforcement that runs outside the model's control. A PreToolUse hook with exit code 2 will ALWAYS block the tool call — no amount of prompt injection can bypass it. The hook runs in your application process, not in the model's context, making it immune to any manipulation of the model's behavior.

why_others_wrong.a: System prompt instructions are probabilistic. The model 'usually' follows them but can deviate. Prompt injection attacks can potentially override instructions. This provides zero guarantee for security-critical operations.
why_others_wrong.b: strict: true validates input structure (JSON Schema compliance) but cannot validate semantic meaning. It can ensure the input is a valid string, but it cannot determine whether that string represents a dangerous command.
why_others_wrong.d: Setting tool_choice: none prevents all tool use, not just dangerous commands. This would block all legitimate operations too, making the agent non-functional.

doc_reference.quote: Exit code 2 from a PreToolUse hook will always block the tool call. No amount of prompt injection can bypass a hook that returns exit code 2. Hooks run outside the model's control.

question: You implement a PreToolUse hook that validates Bash commands for a database agent. The hook script checks for dangerous SQL patterns. During testing, a command that should be blocked gets through. You check the hook script and find it exits with code 1 when detecting a violation. What is the problem?

options.text: Exit code 1 indicates the hook executed successfully, which allows the tool call
Exit code 1 is a NON-BLOCKING error — execution continues. You need exit code 2 to actually block the tool call
Exit code 1 blocks on PreToolUse but not on Bash commands specifically
The hook needs to output JSON with permissionDecision: deny instead of using exit codes

explanation: Exit code 1 is a NON-BLOCKING error — execution continues despite the error. This is one of the most commonly confused facts about hooks. Exit code 0 = success (allow), exit code 1 = non-blocking error (continues), exit code 2 = blocking error (enforces policy). To actually block a tool call, the hook must return exit code 2.

why_others_wrong.a: Exit code 0 indicates success, not exit code 1. Exit code 1 is a non-blocking error, which is different from success but still allows execution to continue.
why_others_wrong.c: Exit code 1 is non-blocking for ALL events and all tool types, not just specific ones. The non-blocking behavior of exit code 1 is universal.
why_others_wrong.d: While JSON with permissionDecision: deny can also block, exit code 2 is the simpler and more direct approach. The issue is specifically that exit code 1 doesn't block — it's not about JSON vs exit codes.

doc_reference.quote: CRITICAL: Exit code 1 does NOT block. Use exit code 2 to enforce policy. Exit codes: 0 = Success, 1 = Non-blocking error (continues execution), 2 = Blocking error.

question: You set up a PostToolUse hook on the Edit tool that runs a linter after every code edit. If the linter finds issues, the hook returns exit code 2, expecting to block the edit. What actually happens?

options.text: The edit is blocked and Claude receives the linter output as an error message
PostToolUse cannot block because the tool has already executed — stderr is shown to Claude as feedback but the edit stands
The edit is reverted and Claude must re-attempt it with the linter feedback
Exit code 2 on PostToolUse triggers a retry of the tool call with modified parameters

explanation: PostToolUse hooks CANNOT block because the tool has already executed by the time the hook runs. Even with exit code 2, the edit has already been applied. The stderr output is shown to Claude as feedback, which Claude can use to make additional edits to fix the issues. But the original edit cannot be undone by the hook.

why_others_wrong.a: PostToolUse cannot block tool execution — the tool has already run. The edit is already applied to the file. PostToolUse hooks provide feedback, not enforcement.
why_others_wrong.c: There is no automatic edit reversion mechanism. The hook cannot undo the edit. Claude receives the feedback and may choose to make corrective edits, but this is a new action, not a revert.
why_others_wrong.d: PostToolUse does not trigger retries. The exit code 2 behavior for PostToolUse is: 'Shows stderr to Claude (tool already ran)' — it doesn't retry or revert.

doc_reference.quote: PostToolUse: After successful tool execution. Can Block? No (tool already ran). Exit 2 behavior: Shows stderr to Claude (tool already ran).

question: You implement a PreToolUse hook that needs to modify a Bash command before execution — adding a --dry-run flag for safety. What must the hook's JSON output include to both allow execution and modify the input?

options.text: Return {"permissionDecision": "allow", "command": "modified command"} at the top level
Return {"hookSpecificOutput": {"hookEventName": "PreToolUse", "permissionDecision": "allow", "updatedInput": {"command": "modified --dry-run"}}} in stdout with exit code 0
Return the modified command as plain text in stdout with exit code 0
Return {"updatedInput": {"command": "modified"}} without permissionDecision — the allow is implied by exit code 0

explanation: To modify tool input via a PreToolUse hook, the JSON output must include hookSpecificOutput with three key fields: hookEventName set to 'PreToolUse', permissionDecision set to 'allow', and updatedInput with the modified parameters. The updatedInput requires permissionDecision: 'allow' and must be inside hookSpecificOutput. The hook returns exit code 0 for success.

why_others_wrong.a: The modified input must be nested inside hookSpecificOutput.updatedInput, not at the top level. The structure is specific and must be followed exactly.
why_others_wrong.c: Plain text output is not parsed as a tool input modification. The hook must return structured JSON with the correct nesting to modify inputs.
why_others_wrong.d: updatedInput requires permissionDecision: 'allow' explicitly — it is not implied by exit code 0. Without the allow decision, the input modification may not be applied.

doc_reference.quote: updatedInput: Modify tool parameters before execution. Requires permissionDecision: 'allow' and must be inside hookSpecificOutput with hookEventName.

question: You want a PreToolUse hook to fire on both Bash and Edit tool calls, but NOT on Read or Grep calls. Which matcher pattern correctly achieves this?

options.text: matcher: "Bash,Edit" — comma-separated list of tool names
matcher: "Bash|Edit" — pipe-separated list matching exact tool names
matcher: "[Bash|Edit]" — bracket-enclosed alternative pattern
matcher: "Bash" and a second matcher: "Edit" — two separate hook entries

explanation: Hook matchers use specific evaluation rules: when the pattern contains only alphanumeric characters, underscores, and pipe characters [a-zA-Z0-9_|], it is treated as an exact string or pipe-separated list. 'Bash|Edit' matches exactly the Bash or Edit tool names. Patterns containing other characters (like comma or brackets) trigger JavaScript regex evaluation instead.

why_others_wrong.a: Comma-separated patterns are not supported in matchers. The comma character would cause the pattern to be evaluated as a JavaScript regex, which would not match tool names correctly.
why_others_wrong.c: Square brackets in the pattern would trigger JavaScript regex evaluation where [Bash|Edit] means a character class, not an alternative — it would match individual characters like 'B', 'a', 's', etc.
why_others_wrong.d: While two separate hook entries would work, it's not the most efficient approach. The pipe-separated pattern 'Bash|Edit' in a single matcher achieves the same result more concisely.

doc_reference.quote: Matcher patterns: Only [a-zA-Z0-9_|] = Exact string or pipe-separated list. Example: Bash, Edit|Write. Contains other chars = JavaScript regex.

question: You want a PreToolUse hook to fire on ALL tools from an MCP memory server. The tools follow the naming pattern mcp__memory__create_entities, mcp__memory__search, etc. Which matcher pattern correctly matches all of them?

options.text: matcher: "mcp__memory"
matcher: "mcp__memory__*"
matcher: "mcp__memory__.*"
matcher: "mcp_memory_*"

explanation: MCP tools follow the naming pattern mcp__<server>__<tool> (double underscores). To match all tools from the memory server, you need mcp__memory__.* — the .* triggers JavaScript regex evaluation and matches any characters after the prefix. Using just * would be treated as a literal asterisk since it contains a non-alphanumeric character that's not in the simple pattern set, but it would not be a proper regex wildcard.

why_others_wrong.a: mcp__memory would only match a tool literally named 'mcp__memory'. It wouldn't match mcp__memory__create_entities or any other specific tool from the server.
why_others_wrong.b: mcp__memory__* contains * which triggers regex evaluation, but in regex * means 'zero or more of the preceding character' (the underscore). It wouldn't correctly match the tool names. You need .* for 'any characters'.
why_others_wrong.d: mcp_memory_* uses single underscores, but MCP tool naming uses double underscores (mcp__memory__tool). This pattern wouldn't match any actual MCP tools.

doc_reference.quote: MCP Tool Naming: mcp__<server>__<tool>. Match all from server: mcp__memory__.* (requires .* for regex).

question: An organization deploys security hooks via managed policy settings. A developer adds 'disableAllHooks: true' in their project's .claude/settings.json. What happens to the managed hooks?

options.text: All hooks are disabled including managed hooks, since disableAllHooks is a global override
User/project settings cannot disable managed hooks — the organizational hooks continue to run regardless
The managed hooks are disabled but a warning is logged to the organization's audit system
The setting is rejected with an error because disableAllHooks is not available at the project level

explanation: User and project settings cannot disable managed hooks. The hierarchy ensures that organization-wide managed hooks always run, regardless of what individual users or projects configure. This is a deliberate security design — administrators can enforce policies that cannot be circumvented by developers.

why_others_wrong.a: disableAllHooks does not override managed hooks. The managed settings hierarchy ensures organizational policies are always enforced.
why_others_wrong.c: Managed hooks are not disabled at all — they continue to run. There is no 'disabled with warning' state.
why_others_wrong.d: disableAllHooks can be set at the project level, but it only affects non-managed hooks. It's not rejected as an error.

doc_reference.quote: Hierarchy: User/project settings cannot disable managed hooks.

question: You need a hook that evaluates whether a tool call is safe by having an LLM analyze the arguments and return a yes/no decision. Which handler type is most appropriate?

options.text: command handler — run a script that calls the LLM API separately
http handler — POST the arguments to an LLM evaluation endpoint
prompt handler — an LLM evaluates a prompt containing the arguments and returns a decision
agent handler — spawns a full agent to investigate the safety of the call

explanation: The prompt handler type is designed exactly for this use case: it has an LLM evaluate a prompt and return a decision. You provide a prompt template with $ARGUMENTS placeholder, specify a model, and the handler returns the LLM's assessment. This is simpler than a command handler calling an API or an agent handler spawning a full agent.

why_others_wrong.a: While a command handler could call an LLM API, it requires you to implement the API call, response parsing, and decision logic in a script. The prompt handler provides this functionality built-in.
why_others_wrong.b: An http handler would require you to build and host an evaluation endpoint. The prompt handler provides LLM evaluation without external infrastructure.
why_others_wrong.d: An agent handler spawns a full verifier agent, which is more heavyweight than needed for a simple yes/no safety check. The prompt handler is sufficient and has a shorter default timeout (30s vs 60s).

doc_reference.quote: Prompt Handler: type: prompt. prompt: 'Review this tool call and return yes/no: $ARGUMENTS'. model: 'claude-opus'. timeout: 30.

question: A security reviewer flags that your hook scripts use relative paths in the settings.json configuration. They recommend switching to absolute paths. What is the security concern with relative paths?

options.text: Relative paths cause hooks to execute more slowly due to path resolution overhead
Relative paths could be exploited if someone places a malicious script in the project directory that matches the relative path
Relative paths are not supported by the hook system and will silently fail
Relative paths break when settings.json is shared across machines with different OS types

explanation: Using relative paths for hook scripts is a security risk because someone could place a malicious script in the project directory that matches the relative path. When the hook runs, it would execute the malicious script instead of your intended one. Absolute paths ensure you always run YOUR script, not a project-supplied one. This is explicitly flagged as a security best practice in the hooks documentation.

why_others_wrong.a: Path resolution overhead is negligible. The concern is security, not performance.
why_others_wrong.c: Relative paths do work in the hook system. The issue is that they create a security vulnerability, not that they fail.
why_others_wrong.d: While absolute paths do create sharing challenges across machines (different home directories), this is a practical concern, not the security concern being raised. The security reviewer's concern is specifically about path hijacking.

doc_reference.source: Skilljar — Claude Code in Action (Gotchas around hooks)
doc_reference.quote: Use absolute paths for scripts — this is a security recommendation. Relative paths could be exploited if someone puts a malicious script in the project.

question: You want to set up an automatic type-checking loop: after Claude edits TypeScript files, a hook runs 'tsc --noEmit', and if there are type errors, Claude sees them and fixes them. Which hook configuration implements this most effectively?

options.text: A PreToolUse hook on Edit that runs tsc before the edit is applied
A PostToolUse hook on Edit that runs tsc and returns any errors as feedback to Claude, who then auto-corrects
A Stop hook that runs tsc when Claude finishes and blocks if errors exist
A SessionEnd hook that runs tsc and reports errors for the next session

explanation: A PostToolUse hook on the Edit tool runs the TypeScript compiler after every edit. While PostToolUse cannot block (the edit is already applied), it feeds the type errors back to Claude as feedback. Claude then automatically fixes the type errors it introduced, creating an auto-correction loop: edit -> type check -> fix errors -> type check again. This is explicitly described as a 'useful hook' pattern in the Skilljar course.

why_others_wrong.a: Running tsc before an edit is applied makes no sense — the edit hasn't happened yet, so tsc would check the old code, not the new code.
why_others_wrong.c: A Stop hook fires when Claude finishes responding, not after individual edits. This would miss intermediate type errors and only catch them at the very end.
why_others_wrong.d: A SessionEnd hook runs when the session terminates. Reporting errors for the 'next session' breaks the auto-correction loop — Claude needs to see and fix errors within the current session.

doc_reference.source: Skilljar — Claude Code in Action (Useful hooks)
doc_reference.quote: PostToolUse hook that triggers after every Edit on TypeScript files. Runs tsc --noEmit. Feeds type errors back to Claude. Claude then automatically fixes the type errors. Auto-correction loop: edit → type check → fix errors → type check again.

question: You need a hook that prevents Claude from reading any .env files. The hook script reads JSON from stdin, checks the file_path argument, and should block the read if the path contains '.env'. What is the correct exit behavior?

options.text: Print 'BLOCKED' to stdout and exit with code 0
Print the error message to stderr and exit with code 1 for a blocking error
Print the error message to stderr and exit with code 2 — stderr with exit 2 is sent to Claude as feedback explaining why the tool was blocked
Return JSON with {"blocked": true} to stdout and exit with code 0

explanation: To block a tool call, the hook must exit with code 2. When exit code 2 is used, the stderr output is sent to Claude as the error message, explaining why the tool was blocked. This allows Claude to understand the restriction and adapt its approach. Exit code 2 ignores stdout JSON and uses stderr as the error message.

why_others_wrong.a: Exit code 0 means success/allow. The tool call would proceed normally despite the 'BLOCKED' message in stdout.
why_others_wrong.b: Exit code 1 is a NON-BLOCKING error. Despite the error message in stderr, execution continues and the Read tool would still access the .env file.
why_others_wrong.d: Exit code 0 means success. JSON with {"blocked": true} would be parsed as debug output but would NOT block the tool call. Only exit code 2 blocks execution.

doc_reference.quote: Exit code 2: Blocking error — Ignores stdout JSON, uses stderr as error message. Stderr output with exit code 2 is sent to Claude as feedback.

question: You want a hook that logs tool usage to an external analytics service without slowing down Claude's operation. Which hook configuration achieves fire-and-forget background execution?

options.text: Set timeout: 1 to make the hook return almost immediately
Set async: true — the hook runs in the background and does NOT block Claude's operation
Use the http handler type which is inherently asynchronous
Set asyncRewake: true to run in the background with Claude waking on completion

explanation: Setting async: true on a hook makes it run in the background without blocking Claude's operation. This is the fire-and-forget pattern — the hook executes independently and Claude continues immediately. It cannot block or modify operations since it runs asynchronously.

why_others_wrong.a: Setting a low timeout doesn't make the hook asynchronous. It still blocks until the timeout expires (or the hook completes), which adds latency to every tool call.
why_others_wrong.c: HTTP handlers are not inherently asynchronous. They block until they receive a response or timeout (default 30 seconds). You need async: true for background execution.
why_others_wrong.d: asyncRewake: true also runs in the background, but Claude wakes on exit code 2 (stderr shown as system reminder). For pure fire-and-forget logging, async: true is more appropriate since you don't need to wake Claude.

doc_reference.quote: async: true — Runs in background, does NOT block. asyncRewake: true — Runs in background, Claude wakes on exit code 2.

question: Three PreToolUse hooks are registered for the Bash tool. Hook A returns permissionDecision: 'allow', Hook B returns permissionDecision: 'ask', and Hook C returns permissionDecision: 'deny'. What is the final outcome?

options.text: The tool is allowed because 'allow' was returned first
The user is prompted because 'ask' is the middle ground between allow and deny
The tool call is blocked because deny has the highest priority — deny > ask > allow
The hooks cancel each other out and the default permission mode applies

explanation: PreToolUse permission decisions follow a strict priority order: deny > ask > allow. If any hook returns 'deny', the operation is blocked regardless of what other hooks return. In this case, Hook C's 'deny' takes precedence over Hook A's 'allow' and Hook B's 'ask'.

why_others_wrong.a: Hook execution order does not determine the outcome. The priority hierarchy (deny > ask > allow) determines the final decision regardless of which hook returned first.
why_others_wrong.b: While 'ask' is between allow and deny, the priority system means deny always wins when present. The user would only be prompted if 'ask' were the highest priority decision returned.
why_others_wrong.d: The hooks do not cancel each other out. The priority system provides a deterministic resolution: the most restrictive decision wins.

doc_reference.quote: Priority Rules: deny > ask > allow — If any hook returns deny, operation is blocked regardless of other hooks.

question: Your PreToolUse hook generates a detailed validation report of 15,000 characters and injects it into Claude's context. What happens to this output?

options.text: The full 15,000 characters are injected into Claude's context without modification
The output is truncated to 10,000 characters — the maximum for hook output injected into context
The output exceeding 10,000 characters is saved to a file and replaced with a preview and path in Claude's context
The hook fails with an error because output exceeded the maximum allowed size

explanation: Hook output injected into context is capped at 10,000 characters. When the output exceeds this limit, it is saved to a file, and Claude's context receives a preview of the content along with the file path. This prevents large hook outputs from consuming excessive context while still making the full content available.

why_others_wrong.a: The output is NOT injected in full. The 10,000 character cap prevents large hook outputs from consuming excessive context.
why_others_wrong.b: The output is not simply truncated. Instead, it's saved to a file with a preview and path provided — preserving the full content while managing context consumption.
why_others_wrong.d: The hook does not fail. The system gracefully handles oversized output by saving it to a file rather than blocking execution.

doc_reference.quote: Output injected into context capped at 10,000 characters. Exceeding limit saves to file, replaced with preview and path.

question: You want to run a setup script each time a specific subagent starts. Which hook event should you use and what does the matcher match against?

options.text: PreToolUse with matcher 'Agent' — fires before the Agent tool spawns the subagent
SubagentStart with the matcher matching the agent type name
SessionStart with the matcher set to the subagent name
TaskCreated with the matcher set to the subagent's task description

explanation: SubagentStart fires when a subagent begins execution, and its matcher matches against the agent type name. This is the correct hook for running setup when a specific subagent starts. You can target specific subagents by setting the matcher to their type name.

why_others_wrong.a: PreToolUse on Agent fires before the Agent tool is invoked, not when the subagent starts executing. The setup script should run when the subagent actually begins, not when the invocation is requested.
why_others_wrong.c: SessionStart fires when a new session starts or resumes, not when a subagent starts. Its matcher matches against startup types (startup, resume, clear, compact), not subagent names.
why_others_wrong.d: TaskCreated fires when a task is created via TaskCreate, which is a different concept from subagent spawning. Tasks are part of the task management system.

doc_reference.quote: SubagentStart/SubagentStop: matches on agent type name. SubagentStart: Subagent spawned. SubagentStop: Subagent finishes.

question: A hook script needs to access the project root directory. Which environment variable is always available to hook scripts for this purpose?

options.text: CLAUDE_HOME — the Claude installation directory
CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR — the project root directory, always available to hooks
PWD — the current working directory at the time of hook execution
CLAUDE_ENV_FILE — a file containing all project environment variables

explanation: CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR is the environment variable that provides the project root directory and is always available to hook scripts. Other variables like CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT and CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA are only available for plugins, and CLAUDE_ENV_FILE is only available for SessionStart, CwdChanged, and FileChanged events.

why_others_wrong.a: CLAUDE_HOME is not a documented hook environment variable. The correct variable for the project directory is CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR.
why_others_wrong.c: While PWD might be set in the shell environment, CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR is the explicitly documented and guaranteed variable for hook scripts to access the project root.
why_others_wrong.d: CLAUDE_ENV_FILE is a file path for persisting environment variables, but it's only available for SessionStart, CwdChanged, and FileChanged events — not always available.

doc_reference.quote: Environment Variables Passed to Hooks: CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR — Project root directory (always).

question: Your team is debating how to enforce a policy that all database queries must be SELECT-only. One engineer proposes adding 'Only use SELECT queries' to the system prompt. Another proposes a PreToolUse hook. Which analysis correctly compares these approaches?

options.text: Both provide the same guarantee level — the system prompt is compiled into deterministic rules at startup
The system prompt provides 100% enforcement since Claude always follows system prompt instructions exactly
The hook provides 100% deterministic enforcement; the system prompt provides only probabilistic guidance that can be bypassed by prompt injection
The system prompt is better because hooks add latency and the security benefit is marginal

explanation: Hooks and prompts operate at fundamentally different guarantee levels. Hooks provide 100% deterministic enforcement — they run outside the model's control and cannot be bypassed by prompt injection. System prompts provide probabilistic guidance — the model 'usually' follows them but can deviate, and prompt injection can potentially override them. For security-critical operations like database access control, hooks are the only approach that provides a guarantee.

why_others_wrong.a: System prompts are NOT compiled into deterministic rules. They provide probabilistic guidance that the model interprets. Only hooks provide deterministic enforcement.
why_others_wrong.b: Claude does NOT always follow system prompt instructions exactly. System prompts are probabilistic — the model can deviate, especially under prompt injection attacks or with ambiguous instructions.
why_others_wrong.d: The security benefit is not marginal. For database access control, the difference between probabilistic guidance and deterministic enforcement is critical. A prompt injection that bypasses a system prompt instruction could lead to data modification or deletion.

doc_reference.quote: Hooks: 100% — code enforced. Bypass risk: None (runs outside model). Prompts: Best effort — model may deviate. Bypass risk: Prompt injection possible.

question: You are designing a defense-in-depth strategy for a tool that executes SQL queries. Which ordering of enforcement layers follows the recommended approach from weakest to strongest?

options.text: Hooks (block dangerous queries) -> strict: true (validate schema) -> System prompt (guide behavior)
System prompt (guide toward SELECT queries) -> strict: true (validate query is a string) -> Hooks (validate query contains only SELECT)
strict: true (validate schema) -> System prompt (guide behavior) -> Hooks (block dangerous queries)
All three layers provide identical guarantees and ordering doesn't matter

explanation: Defense in depth combines three layers from probabilistic to deterministic: (1) System prompts shape behavior — the first line that guides intent, (2) strict: true ensures tool inputs are structurally valid — the second line that validates format, (3) Hooks enforce hard security boundaries — the final line that blocks violations. This ordering means even if the probabilistic layer fails, the deterministic layers catch violations.

why_others_wrong.a: This reverses the recommended ordering. The layers should go from weakest (prompts — probabilistic) to strongest (hooks — deterministic), not the other way around.
why_others_wrong.c: This puts strict: true first and system prompt second, which doesn't follow the recommended progression. The documentation specifies: prompts first (shape intent), strict second (validate format), hooks third (enforce policy).
why_others_wrong.d: The three layers provide fundamentally different guarantee levels. Prompts are probabilistic, strict: true is deterministic for schema, and hooks are deterministic for semantics. Ordering matters for defense in depth.

doc_reference.quote: Prompts guide Claude toward correct behavior (first line — shapes intent). strict: true ensures tool inputs are structurally valid (second line). Hooks enforce hard security boundaries (final line — blocks violations).

question: A developer needs to share a hook configuration with their team via version control. They also have a personal hook for local debugging that should not be shared. Which settings file combination achieves this?

options.text: Both hooks in ~/.claude/settings.json — gitignore the file to prevent accidental commits
Shared hook in .claude/settings.json (committed to repo); personal hook in .claude/settings.local.json (gitignored)
Shared hook in .claude/settings.local.json; personal hook in ~/.claude/settings.json
Both hooks in .claude/settings.json with a 'personal' flag on the debug hook

explanation: The hook configuration system provides three levels with different shareability: .claude/settings.json is project-scoped and shareable (commit to repo), .claude/settings.local.json is project-scoped but NOT shareable (gitignored), and ~/.claude/settings.json is user-scoped (not shareable, local machine). The correct approach is shared hooks in .claude/settings.json and personal hooks in .claude/settings.local.json.

why_others_wrong.a: ~/.claude/settings.json applies to ALL projects, not just this one. Putting project-specific shared hooks there would affect the developer's other projects too.
why_others_wrong.c: This reverses the correct usage. .claude/settings.local.json is gitignored (not shareable), and ~/.claude/settings.json is user-wide (not project-specific).
why_others_wrong.d: There is no 'personal' flag on hooks. The shareability is determined by which settings file the hook is placed in, not by a flag on the hook itself.

doc_reference.quote: Settings File Locations: .claude/settings.json — Single project, Yes (commit to repo). .claude/settings.local.json — Single project, No (gitignored). ~/.claude/settings.json — All projects, No (local machine).

question: Your team wants to share hook configurations via version control, but developers use different machines with different home directories. The Skilljar course recommends a specific solution. What is it?

options.text: Use environment variable expansion in settings.json directly, like $HOME/scripts/hook.sh
Create a settings.example.json with $PWD placeholder and an init script that generates the local settings.json with absolute paths
Use Docker containers to standardize paths across all developer machines
Store hooks as npm scripts in package.json so paths are resolved via node_modules

explanation: The Skilljar course specifically recommends creating a settings.example.json template with $PWD placeholder for paths. An init/setup script then replaces $PWD with the actual absolute path, generating each developer's local settings.json. This solves the tension between absolute paths (required for security) and cross-machine sharing.

why_others_wrong.a: Settings.json does not support environment variable expansion in hook command paths. The $HOME syntax would be treated as a literal string, not expanded.
why_others_wrong.c: Docker containers are a heavy-handed solution for path standardization. The settings.example.json pattern is simpler and more appropriate for this specific problem.
why_others_wrong.d: Storing hooks as npm scripts doesn't solve the absolute path requirement for settings.json. The hook configuration still needs absolute paths to the actual scripts.

doc_reference.source: Skilljar — Claude Code in Action (Gotchas around hooks)
doc_reference.quote: Solution: use $PWD placeholder in a settings.example.json template. An init/setup script replaces $PWD with the actual absolute path. Each developer runs the init script to generate their local settings.json.

question: You need to design a hook-based quality assurance system. After Claude edits files, a linter runs automatically, and if issues are found, Claude gets the feedback and fixes them. A colleague suggests using PreToolUse instead of PostToolUse. Why is PostToolUse the better choice here?

options.text: PreToolUse hooks are slower than PostToolUse hooks due to the blocking behavior
PreToolUse runs before the edit is applied — the linter would check the old code, not Claude's new code. PostToolUse runs after the edit, so the linter checks the actual changes
PreToolUse hooks cannot access the tool input parameters needed for linting
PreToolUse hooks are reserved for security enforcement and cannot be used for quality checks

explanation: PreToolUse runs BEFORE the tool executes — at that point, the edit hasn't been applied yet, so a linter would check the old (pre-edit) code and find no issues related to Claude's changes. PostToolUse runs AFTER the edit is applied, meaning the linter checks the actual modified files. This is the correct timing for quality feedback.

why_others_wrong.a: The speed difference between PreToolUse and PostToolUse hooks is not the concern. The issue is timing: when to run the linter relative to the file edit.
why_others_wrong.c: PreToolUse hooks DO have access to tool input parameters. They can see the tool name, arguments, and all input details. The issue is timing, not data access.
why_others_wrong.d: PreToolUse hooks can be used for any purpose, not just security. However, for linting after edits, PostToolUse is the correct timing choice.

doc_reference.source: Skilljar — Claude Code in Action (Useful hooks)
doc_reference.quote: PostToolUse hook on Edit tool: runs after edit is applied, feeds errors back to Claude, who then automatically fixes them.

question: A developer creates a subagent and expects it to receive the full Claude Code system prompt plus the custom body from the markdown file. During testing, the subagent behaves differently from the main conversation. What is the likely explanation?

options.text: Subagents receive ONLY their custom system prompt (the markdown body) plus basic environment details — NOT the full Claude Code system prompt
Subagents receive the full Claude Code system prompt but with the custom body appended at the end
Subagents receive a compressed version of the Claude Code system prompt to save context
Subagents receive the same prompt as the main conversation but with tool restrictions applied

explanation: Subagents receive ONLY their custom system prompt (from the markdown body) plus basic environment details (working directory, platform, shell, OS). They do NOT receive the full Claude Code system prompt. This is an important design consideration — if the subagent needs specific behaviors, those must be explicitly stated in its markdown body.

why_others_wrong.b: The full Claude Code system prompt is NOT included. Subagents get only their own custom prompt, which is a complete replacement, not an append.
why_others_wrong.c: There is no compression. The Claude Code system prompt is simply not given to subagents at all. They get their own prompt only.
why_others_wrong.d: Subagents do NOT receive the parent's system prompt. They receive an entirely separate prompt defined in their markdown file.

doc_reference.quote: The body becomes the system prompt. Subagents receive ONLY this system prompt (plus basic environment details), NOT the full Claude Code system prompt.

question: You configure allowed_domains on a web_search tool to restrict searches to your company's documentation. You set allowed_domains: ['docs.example.com']. A test query returns results from 'docs.example.com/api/v2/reference'. Why does this work?

options.text: The domain filter only checks the base domain and ignores all path components
Subpaths are automatically included — 'example.com/blog' matches 'example.com/blog/post-1', and subdomains are also included
The allowed_domains field supports regex patterns, and 'docs.example.com' is interpreted as a wildcard
Results from any path on the domain are returned but marked as unverified

explanation: Domain filtering automatically includes subpaths and subdomains. Specifying 'example.com' covers 'docs.example.com' and 'example.com/blog/post-1'. Specifying 'docs.example.com' covers all paths under that subdomain. Domains should NOT include HTTP/HTTPS scheme, and you cannot use both allowed_domains and blocked_domains simultaneously.

why_others_wrong.a: The domain filter doesn't just check the base domain. It also supports subpath matching — 'example.com/blog' would match 'example.com/blog/post-1' but not 'example.com/about'.
why_others_wrong.c: allowed_domains does not support regex. It uses simple domain and subpath matching with limited wildcard support (one * per entry, must appear after the domain part in path).
why_others_wrong.d: Results from matching paths are returned normally, not marked as unverified. The filtering is binary: matching domains are allowed, non-matching domains are blocked.

doc_reference.quote: Subdomains automatically included (example.com covers docs.example.com). Subpaths supported (example.com/blog matches example.com/blog/post-1).

question: In the Agent SDK, you set permissionMode: 'bypassPermissions' and also set allowed_tools: ['Read', 'Grep']. A colleague assumes this restricts the agent to only Read and Grep. Why is this assumption dangerous?

options.text: bypassPermissions ignores allowed_tools entirely and auto-approves EVERY tool. Use disallowed_tools to block specific tools in bypassPermissions mode
allowed_tools is not available in the Agent SDK, only in the CLI
bypassPermissions requires explicit tool listing, but the syntax should be allowedTools not allowed_tools
The assumption is correct — allowed_tools properly restricts bypassPermissions to only those tools

explanation: This is a critical security warning from the documentation: allowed_tools does NOT constrain bypassPermissions. Setting allowed_tools=['Read'] alongside bypassPermissions still approves EVERY tool. To block specific tools in bypassPermissions mode, use disallowed_tools instead, which actively removes tools from the available set.

why_others_wrong.b: allowed_tools is available in the Agent SDK. The issue is not availability but the fact that it doesn't constrain bypassPermissions mode.
why_others_wrong.c: The naming convention (snake_case in Python, camelCase in TypeScript) is correct but irrelevant. The fundamental issue is that allowed_tools doesn't restrict bypassPermissions regardless of naming.
why_others_wrong.d: This assumption is WRONG and dangerous. bypassPermissions approves all tools regardless of allowed_tools, potentially giving the agent access to destructive operations.

doc_reference.quote: Critical Warning: allowed_tools does NOT constrain bypassPermissions. Setting allowed_tools=['Read'] alongside bypassPermissions still approves EVERY tool. Use disallowed_tools to block specific tools.

question: A subagent definition includes 'skills: api-conventions, error-handling-patterns' in the frontmatter. How are these skills loaded compared to skills in the main conversation?

options.text: Skills are just listed as available for the subagent to invoke via the Skill tool when needed
Full skill content is injected into the subagent's context at startup — not just made available for invocation. Subagents do NOT inherit skills from the parent
Skills are inherited from the parent conversation and the frontmatter list is additive
Only skill metadata is loaded; the full content is fetched when the subagent calls the skill

explanation: When skills are listed in a subagent's frontmatter, the full skill content is injected into the subagent's context at startup — not just made available for lazy invocation. This is important because subagents don't inherit skills from the parent conversation. You must explicitly list any skills the subagent needs in its frontmatter.

why_others_wrong.a: Skills listed in the frontmatter are not just listed for invocation — their full content is immediately injected into the subagent's context. This is different from skills in the main conversation which use progressive disclosure.
why_others_wrong.c: Subagents do NOT inherit skills from the parent conversation. Skills must be explicitly listed in the subagent's frontmatter. There is no inheritance or additive behavior.
why_others_wrong.d: Full skill content is loaded at startup, not lazily fetched. The frontmatter skills field triggers immediate injection of the complete skill content into the subagent's context.

doc_reference.quote: Full skill content is injected into subagent's context at startup, not just available for invocation. Subagents don't inherit skills from parent conversation; must list explicitly.

question: You need a subagent that can only execute read-only database queries via Bash. You implement a PreToolUse hook in the subagent's frontmatter that validates SQL commands and exits with code 2 for write operations. During testing from a plugin, the hook doesn't fire. What went wrong?

options.text: Hooks in subagent frontmatter only fire for PostToolUse events, not PreToolUse
The subagent was loaded from a plugin, and plugin subagents silently ignore hooks — copy it to .claude/agents/ to enable hooks
PreToolUse hooks require the hook script to be registered in settings.json, not in frontmatter
Frontmatter hooks only fire when the agent runs as the main session via --agent, not when spawned as a subagent

explanation: Plugin subagents silently ignore hooks, mcpServers, and permissionMode frontmatter fields for security reasons. If the subagent was loaded from a plugin's agents/ directory, its hooks would not fire. The solution is to copy the agent file to .claude/agents/ (project scope) or ~/.claude/agents/ (user scope), where these features are fully supported.

why_others_wrong.a: Frontmatter hooks support both PreToolUse and PostToolUse events. The issue is the plugin source, not the event type.
why_others_wrong.c: Frontmatter hooks are valid for subagent definitions. PreToolUse hooks can be defined in frontmatter and fire when the subagent uses tools. The issue is specifically that plugin-sourced subagents ignore hooks.
why_others_wrong.d: The opposite is true: frontmatter hooks fire when spawned as a subagent through the Agent tool or @-mention. They do NOT fire when running as main session via --agent.

doc_reference.quote: Plugin subagents do NOT support hooks, mcpServers, or permissionMode frontmatter fields (silently ignored). To use them, copy the agent file into .claude/agents/ or ~/.claude/agents/.

question: You are creating a custom tool for the Agent SDK. The tool handler throws an uncaught exception during execution. What happens to the agent loop?

options.text: Claude receives the exception as an error message and can retry with different parameters
The agent loop STOPS entirely — Claude never sees the error. You should return isError: true instead
The exception is caught and converted to a generic 'tool execution failed' message for Claude
The SDK automatically retries the tool call up to 3 times before stopping

explanation: In the Agent SDK, an uncaught exception from a tool handler stops the agent loop entirely — Claude never sees the error and cannot recover. Instead, you should catch exceptions in your handler and return {isError: true, content: [{type: 'text', text: 'error description'}]}. This keeps the loop running and gives Claude the information to adapt its approach.

why_others_wrong.a: Claude does NOT see uncaught exceptions. The agent loop terminates before Claude can process the error. Only returned isError: true results reach Claude.
why_others_wrong.c: The SDK does NOT automatically convert exceptions to error messages. Uncaught exceptions cause the loop to stop, period.
why_others_wrong.d: There is no automatic retry for uncaught exceptions. The loop stops immediately. Claude's 2-3 retry behavior applies to invalid tool CALLS (wrong parameters), not to execution failures.

doc_reference.quote: Uncaught exception: Agent loop STOPS. Claude never sees the error. Return isError: true: Agent loop CONTINUES. Claude sees error and can retry/adapt.

question: In the Agent SDK, which factor determines whether a custom tool runs in parallel with other tools or sequentially?

options.text: The order in which tools are listed in the allowedTools configuration
The readOnlyHint annotation — tools marked readOnlyHint: true run concurrently, while state-modifying tools run sequentially
All custom tools always run sequentially regardless of configuration
The permissionMode setting — bypassPermissions enables parallel execution

explanation: In the Agent SDK, tool parallelization depends on the readOnlyHint annotation. Built-in read-only tools (Read, Glob, Grep) run concurrently by default. State-modifying tools (Edit, Write, Bash) run sequentially. Custom tools default to sequential execution; mark them with readOnlyHint: true to enable parallel execution.

why_others_wrong.a: The order in allowedTools determines which tools are available, not whether they run in parallel. Parallelization is controlled by the readOnlyHint annotation.
why_others_wrong.c: Custom tools can run in parallel when marked with readOnlyHint: true. They default to sequential, but this can be explicitly changed.
why_others_wrong.d: Permission mode controls approval flow, not execution parallelization. The readOnlyHint annotation is the mechanism for controlling parallel vs sequential execution.

doc_reference.quote: Read-only tools (Read, Glob, Grep, read-only MCP tools) run concurrently. State-modifying tools (Edit, Write, Bash) run sequentially. Custom tools default to sequential; mark readOnlyHint: true for parallel.

question: You want to create an agent coordinator that restricts which subagent types can be spawned. The coordinator should only be able to spawn 'worker' and 'researcher' subagents. Which frontmatter syntax achieves this when the coordinator runs as the main session with --agent?

options.text: disallowedTools: Agent — prevents spawning all subagents except whitelisted ones
tools: Agent(worker, researcher), Read, Bash — allows spawning only worker and researcher subagent types
allowedAgents: worker, researcher — a dedicated field for restricting subagent types
tools: Agent, Read, Bash with a PreToolUse hook filtering by subagent type

explanation: The Agent(agent_type) syntax in the tools field creates an allowlist of which subagent types can be spawned. 'Agent(worker, researcher)' means only worker and researcher subagents can be spawned. This syntax only applies to agents running as the main thread with --agent. 'Agent' without parentheses allows spawning any subagent. Omitting Agent entirely prevents spawning.

why_others_wrong.a: disallowedTools: Agent would completely remove the Agent tool, preventing ALL subagent spawning. It doesn't support selective whitelisting of subagent types.
why_others_wrong.c: There is no 'allowedAgents' frontmatter field. The Agent(type) syntax in the tools field provides this functionality.
why_others_wrong.d: While a PreToolUse hook could filter subagent types, the Agent(agent_type) syntax provides this as a built-in feature without the overhead of implementing and maintaining a hook.

doc_reference.quote: Agent(worker, researcher) in tools field — Restrict which subagent types can be spawned. Agent without parentheses — Allow spawning any subagent. Agent omitted — Cannot spawn subagents.

question: You configure a subagent with initialPrompt: 'Analyze the current project structure and suggest improvements' in its frontmatter. When you invoke this subagent from the main conversation via @mention, the initial prompt doesn't execute. When does initialPrompt apply?

options.text: initialPrompt runs every time the subagent is invoked, regardless of how
initialPrompt only applies when the agent runs as the main session agent via --agent or the agent setting — not when spawned as a subagent
initialPrompt only applies on the first invocation per session, then is discarded
initialPrompt requires background: true to be set simultaneously

explanation: initialPrompt is auto-submitted as the first user turn ONLY when the agent runs as the main session agent (via --agent flag or agent setting in project config). It does not apply when the agent is spawned as a subagent via the Agent tool or @-mention. When used with --agent, the initialPrompt is prepended to any user-provided prompt.

why_others_wrong.a: initialPrompt does NOT run for every invocation. It specifically applies only when the agent is the main session agent, not when spawned as a subagent.
why_others_wrong.c: The 'first invocation per session' concept is not how initialPrompt works. It applies based on HOW the agent is invoked (main session vs subagent), not when.
why_others_wrong.d: initialPrompt does not require background: true. These are independent configuration fields.

doc_reference.quote: initialPrompt: Auto-submitted as first user turn when agent runs as main session agent (via --agent or agent setting). Commands and skills are processed. Prepended to any user-provided prompt.

question: A developer needs to add code review capability. They are deciding between creating a skill and creating a subagent. The code review generates extensive file analysis (thousands of tokens of intermediate output) but only needs a summary. Which option is better and why?

options.text: A skill — because skills can be invoked lazily and only load when triggered
A subagent — because it runs in isolated context, the extensive intermediate analysis stays out of the main context, and only the summary returns
A skill — because skills support all tools while subagents have restricted tool access
Either would work identically since both provide context isolation

explanation: This is the core use case for subagents over skills. Skills run in the main conversation context — all that intermediate file analysis (thousands of tokens) would accumulate in the main context. Subagents run in their own isolated context window, so the extensive analysis stays there and only the summary returns. The Skilljar course specifically states: 'Use a subagent when a side task would flood your main conversation with search results, logs, or file contents you won't reference again.'

why_others_wrong.a: While skills do have progressive disclosure (lazy loading), they run IN the main conversation context. The extensive intermediate output would still consume main context tokens.
why_others_wrong.c: Both skills and subagents can access tools. Subagents inherit all tools by default if the tools field is omitted. The distinction is about context isolation, not tool access.
why_others_wrong.d: They do NOT work identically. Skills run in the caller's context (no isolation), while subagents run in isolated context. This is a fundamental architectural difference.

doc_reference.quote: Use a subagent when a side task would flood your main conversation with search results, logs, or file contents you won't reference again. Skills: run in the main conversation context. Subagents: run in isolated context, only summary returns.

question: You add critical behavioral rules to the initial prompt of an Agent SDK query. After a long conversation, the agent stops following these rules. What is the most likely cause and the recommended fix?

options.text: The model has a behavior drift bug — report it to Anthropic support
The rules were lost during automatic compaction. Persistent rules should go in CLAUDE.md (re-injected every request), not the initial prompt
The context window size was reduced, removing the initial prompt. Increase max_tokens to prevent this
The agent's permission mode was changed mid-session, overriding the behavioral rules

explanation: When automatic compaction occurs, older conversation history is summarized to free context. The initial prompt may be lost or summarized during compaction. The Agent SDK documentation specifically warns: 'Persistent rules belong in CLAUDE.md (re-injected every request), not initial prompt (may be lost on compaction).' CLAUDE.md content is re-injected into every request regardless of compaction.

why_others_wrong.a: This is not a model bug. It's an expected consequence of how compaction works — the initial prompt is part of the conversation history that gets summarized.
why_others_wrong.c: max_tokens controls per-response output limits, not the context window. Compaction is triggered by context approaching its limit, not by max_tokens settings.
why_others_wrong.d: Permission mode changes affect tool approval, not behavioral rules. The behavioral drift is caused by compaction losing the initial prompt content.

doc_reference.quote: Important: Persistent rules belong in CLAUDE.md (re-injected every request), not initial prompt (may be lost on compaction).

question: Your long-running subagent processes extensive codebases and approaches its context limit. What auto-compaction behavior applies to subagents?

options.text: Subagents do not support compaction — they terminate when their context is full
Subagents support auto-compaction using the same logic as the main conversation, triggering at approximately 95% capacity by default
Subagent compaction is handled by the parent conversation's compaction process
Subagents automatically transfer their context to the parent when full, triggering parent compaction

explanation: Subagents support automatic compaction using the same logic as the main conversation. Auto-compaction triggers at approximately 95% capacity by default (configurable via CLAUDE_AUTOCOMPACT_PCT_OVERRIDE). Importantly, subagent transcripts are stored in separate files and are NOT affected by main conversation compaction — they are independent.

why_others_wrong.a: Subagents DO support compaction. They use the same auto-compaction mechanism as the main conversation to manage their independent context windows.
why_others_wrong.c: Subagent compaction is NOT handled by the parent. Each subagent manages its own context independently, with its own compaction events.
why_others_wrong.d: Subagents do not transfer context to the parent. Context isolation is maintained — the subagent handles its own compaction and only the final summary returns to the parent.

doc_reference.quote: Subagents support automatic compaction using same logic as main conversation. Default trigger: approximately 95% capacity. Override: set CLAUDE_AUTOCOMPACT_PCT_OVERRIDE. Main conversation compaction: subagent transcripts are unaffected.
